meta.description: Published candidate crash/impact sites for MH370. Every lat/lon was fetched from the cited source in this session; no memory-only coordinates. These are mutually inconsistent hypotheses, not corroborating evidence: each derives from a different methodology and they span ~6 degrees of latitude along and off the 7th arc.
sources: UGIB 2020 paper PDF pp.52, 57 ('the LEP is S34.2342° E93.7875°'), via https://mh370.radiantphysics.com/papers/

CSIRO, 'The search for MH370 and ocean surface drift - Part III' (Griffin & Oke, report EP174155, 26 June 2017), https://www.atsb.gov.au/sites/default/files/media/5773371/mh370_csiro-ocean-drift-iiil.pdf - pp.6, 28: 'This location is 35.6°S, 92.8°E', with lower-likelihood west-of-arc alternatives 'near 34.7°S 92.6°E and 35.3°S 91.8°E'

ATSB, 'MH370 - First Principles Review' (AE-2014-054, report of 2-4 Nov 2016 meeting, published 20 Dec 2016), https://www.atsb.gov.au/sites/default/files/media/5772107/ae2014054_final-first-principles-report.pdf - pp.3, 23: remaining ~25,000 km2 area of high probability between latitudes 32.5S and 36S along the 7th arc, within ~25 NM of the arc

Richard Godfrey, 'MH370 Search Area (Updated)', mh370search.com, 22 Dec 2023: 'The WSPR search area is a circle with a 30 km radius centred on 29.128°S 99.934°E at a depth of 3,750 m'; earlier GDTAAA preliminary finding (30 Nov 2021) was 33.177S 95.300E
Ashton et al. (2015), 'The Search for MH370', Journal of Navigation 68(1), Table 9 final row (p.20) and conclusions (p.22): 'indicating a last contact location of 34.7°S and 93.0°E'
notes: status vocabulary: searched-2014-2017 (ATSB deep-tow/AUV corridor), searched-2018 (Ocean Infinity Seabed Constructor), partially-searched, unsearched, being-searched-2025-26.

atsb-fpr-2016 is the only site whose lat/lon is DERIVED rather than published: the FPR publishes an area (32.5S-36S, ~25 NM either side of the 7th arc, ~25,000 km2) but no centre coordinate; lat is the midpoint of the published latitude extent and lon is this repo's calibrated 7th-arc model longitude at that latitude (same model as search-areas.json, expect up to ~0.5 deg lon error).
Distances from the 7th arc quoted in notes use the same approximate arc model.

The Inmarsat 'hotspot' popularised by The Economist (2015) is the terminal point of the Ashton et al. example path; the paper itself stresses the route 'is no more valid than many other potential routes'.

sites.name: UGIB 2020 Last Estimated Position (LEP)
sites.publishedBy: Bobby Ulich, Richard Godfrey, Victor Iannello, Andrew Banks

citation.label: 'The Final Resting Place of MH370' (UGIB 2020), pp.52, 57: 'the LEP is S34.2342° E93.7875°'

sites.methodology: SATCOM BTO/BFO route fitting over all candidate LNAV routes, combined with fuel-range/endurance, debris-drift and aerial-search probabilities; best-fit route is LNAV 180.0 deg true from waypoint BEDAX toward the South Pole at FL390/LRC, impact on the 7th arc at the BEDAX longitude
sites.note: On the 7th arc inside the 2014-17 ATSB corridor and the 2018 OI area, but UGIB (p.52) identify unscanned terrain incl. a steep slope ~20 NM south of the LEP. The point anchors the Ocean Infinity 2025-26 search zone (33S-36S).

sites.name: CSIRO drift-analysis most-likely location (Part III)
sites.publishedBy: CSIRO Oceans and Atmosphere (David Griffin, Peter Oke), for the ATSB

citation.label: 'The search for MH370 and ocean surface drift - Part III', report EP174155, pp.6, 28: 'the location of the aircraft, with unprecedented precision and certainty. This location is 35.6°S, 92.8°E.'

sites.methodology: Forward drift modelling of recovered debris and of objects in March 2014 Pleiades satellite imagery (image PHR4), constrained by the 2014 surface search, absence of Australian-coast debris, and arrival timing of African-shore debris
sites.note: The report also allows lower-likelihood west-of-arc alternatives near 34.7S 92.6E and 35.3S 91.8E. The 35.6S region was covered by Ocean Infinity in 2018 without a find; parts are being re-examined in the wider 2025-26 zone.

sites.name: ATSB First Principles Review remaining priority area (indicative centre)
sites.publishedBy: ATSB First Principles Review expert panel (with DST Group, CSIRO, Boeing, Inmarsat, DCA Malaysia and others)

citation.label: ATSB, 'MH370 - First Principles Review' (AE-2014-054), pp.3, 23: ~25,000 km2 remaining high-probability area between latitudes 32.5S and 36S along the 7th arc, within ~25 NM (99% of DST Group paths)

sites.methodology: Expert review combining DST Group Bayesian SATCOM analysis, completed-search elimination (high confidence the 120,000 km2 area does not contain the aircraft), end-of-flight/flap analysis (impact within ~25 NM of arc) and CSIRO drift results (region near 35S most consistent)
sites.note: DERIVED CENTRE, not a published coordinate: the FPR publishes only the area bounds; lat is the midpoint of 32.5S-36S and lon is the repo arc-model longitude at that latitude (see meta.notes). Ocean Infinity searched this 25,000 km2 area first in 2018 without a find.

sites.name: Godfrey GDTAAA/WSPR candidate site

citation.label: 'MH370 Search Area (Updated)', mh370search.com, 22 Dec 2023: 'The WSPR search area is a circle with a 30 km radius centred on 29.128°S 99.934°E at a depth of 3,750 m'

sites.methodology: WSPR-based (Weak Signal Propagation Reporter): claimed detection and tracking of the aircraft via anomalies in amateur-radio propagation links (GDTAAA software), cross-referenced with Inmarsat arcs, fuel and drift analyses. DISPUTED METHODOLOGY: the physical basis (aircraft scatter detectable in global WSPR logs) is rejected by several MH370 analysts, including UGIB co-author Victor Iannello
sites.note: About 43 NM east of the 7th arc at 29.1S per the repo arc model - outside every searched corridor; also lies in the latitude band contra-indicated by the CSIRO drift analysis (impact 'likely not north of 32S'). An earlier GDTAAA preliminary finding (30 Nov 2021) was 33.177S 95.300E, 6 NM east of the arc; the 29.128S location is the later refinement publicised 2021-2023. The full WSPR/GDTAAA candidate track is plotted as the wspr-gdtaaa-2023 Epoch 3 reconstruction. Still unsearched after the 2025-26 Ocean Infinity campaign, which targeted the 33-36S zone and ended Jan 2026 without a find (Godfrey's 2023 paper claims ~46% of his 70x40 NM crash area was previously searched).

sites.name: Inmarsat example-path terminus ('hotspot', Ashton et al. 2015)
sites.publishedBy: Inmarsat (Chris Ashton, Alan Shuster Bruce, Gary Colledge, Mark Dickinson)

citation.label: Ashton et al. (2015), 'The Search for MH370', Journal of Navigation 68(1), Table 9 final row 00:19:29 (p.20) and conclusions (p.22): 'a last contact location of 34.7°S and 93.0°E'

sites.methodology: BTO/BFO example reconstruction: constant 450 kt ground-speed route crossing the 03:41 (UTC+8) arc at 0N, matching BFO within 2 Hz at the five post-03:41 (UTC+8) signalling epochs; the paper stresses 'significant uncertainty in the final location'
sites.note: ~9 NM west of the 7th arc at 34.7S per the repo arc model, inside the ATSB 120,000 km2 corridor searched 2014-17. Widely reported in 2015 media (e.g. The Economist) as the Inmarsat 'hotspot'.

sites.name: CAPTIO 2022 northernmost Point of Impact

citation.label: Blelly & Marchand (CAPTIO v4a, 2022), 'Analysis of Flight MH370', Executive Summary p.1: 'The northernmost probable point of impact (POI) ... is located at [35 deg 39' S; 93 deg 01' E]'; crash zone -35.6650 S 93.0130 E to -36.0269 S 93.0364 E (sections 2.8-2.9)

sites.methodology: Piloted-flight reconstruction: constant FL300 / Mach 0.706 (ICAO holding speed) southern leg fitting all 7 arcs plus 3 extra Inmarsat constraints (02:28 (UTC+8) BTO + 02:40 (UTC+8)/07:14 (UTC+8) BFO), followed by a manual engine shutdown, APU-powered glide and controlled ditching south of the 7th arc. The POI is the northern end of a ~22 NM crash-zone segment; the reconstructed track is the captio-2022 Epoch 3 path.
sites.note: The terminus of the CAPTIO reconstruction, ~55 NM SW of the UGIB LEP. Because CAPTIO model a powered glide PAST the 7th arc, the POI lies ~45 NM beyond (SW of) the arc - outside the classic 'within ~25 NM' constraint. Much of the surrounding 33-36 S band was covered by the failed 2025-26 Ocean Infinity search; CAPTIO propose a specific southern extension (see the captio-blelly-marchand-proposed search zone) they argue was not fully scanned.

sites.name: DSTG 2015 Bayesian hotspot (~38 deg S)
sites.publishedBy: Australian Defence Science and Technology Group (DSTG)

citation.label: DSTG, 'Bayesian Methods in the Search for MH370' (ATSB, 2015): posterior probability density along the 7th arc peaking near 38 deg S

sites.methodology: Bayesian filter combining a radar-derived prior for the 02:22 (UTC+8) position and speed, an aircraft-dynamics motion model, and the Inmarsat BTO/BFO measurements at each handshake; the posterior probability density along the 7th arc peaks near 38 deg S at the 08:19 (UTC+8) final handshake. Longitude snapped to the rendered FL350 7th arc at that latitude.
sites.note: The formal statistical basis for the original ATSB priority zone. The 2014-17 wide underwater search covered this southern band and found nothing, which (together with drift modelling) shifted later estimates north: CSIRO ~35.6 deg S, UGIB ~34.2 deg S.
